title: Who May Practice Before the IRS
description: Understand who is authorized to represent taxpayers before the IRS

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: As an Enrolled Agent, you'll have the right to represent taxpayers before the IRS. Understanding who can practice, what 'practice' means, and the different levels of authorization is fundamental to your professional role.

sections.title: Practice Before the IRS Defined
sections.content:
**What is Practice Before the IRS?**

All matters connected with a presentation to the IRS or any of its officers or employees relating to a taxpayer's rights, privileges, or liabilities under laws or regulations administered by the IRS.

**Includes:**
• Preparing documents
• Filing documents
• Corresponding and communicating with the IRS
• Rendering written advice about the above
• Representing a client at conferences, hearings, and meetings

sections.title: Practitioners with Unlimited Practice Rights
rows: Pass SEE or IRS experience, background check, continuing education
Member in good standing of state bar

sections.title: Limited Practice Rights
sections.content:
**Unenrolled Return Preparer:**
Can represent taxpayers ONLY:
• Before revenue agents, customer service reps, and similar IRS personnel
• For returns THEY prepared and signed
• Not for Appeals, Collections, Counsel, or other offices

**Annual Filing Season Program (AFSP):**
Unenrolled tax Return preparers who complete annual requirements:
• Limited representation rights (same as unenrolled)
• Listed in IRS directory
• Must complete continuing education annually

**Students/LITC Participants:**
Can represent in limited circumstances with practitioner supervision

sections.title: Special Appearances
sections.content:
**Taxpayer May Represent Self:**
Any individual can represent themselves.

**Others Who May Appear:**
• Family members (with some restrictions)
• Officers/employees of corporations
• General partners of partnerships
• Trustees, administrators of trusts/estates
• Witnesses

**Power of Attorney Not Required:**
For appearing as a witness or providing information

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: EA Practice Rights
sections.content:
**EAs have "Unlimited" practice rights meaning:**

• ANY tax matter
• ANY IRS office (including Appeals, Collections, Counsel)
• ANY taxpayer (not just returns you prepared)
• ANY tax year or period

**Same as attorneys and CPAs - no limitations!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Practice = representing taxpayers in IRS matters
EAs, CPAs, and Attorneys have unlimited practice rights
Enrolled Retirement Plan Agents limited to retirement plan matters
Unenrolled preparers can only represent on returns they prepared
AFSP provides limited representation with annual CE requirements
Taxpayers can always represent themselves
Corporate officers can represent their corporations

title: Power of Attorney and Authorizations
description: Master the forms and requirements for representing taxpayers

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: You can't represent a client without proper authorization! Form 2848 is your key to acting on behalf of clients. Understanding when and how to use each authorization form is essential to your practice.

sections.title: Form 2848: Power of Attorney
sections.content:
**What Form 2848 Authorizes:**

• Represent the taxpayer before the IRS
• Receive confidential tax information
• Perform all acts the taxpayer can perform (unless restricted)
• Sign certain documents on behalf of taxpayer

**Key Elements:**
• Specific tax matters (tax form, years/periods)
• Representative's CAF number
• Taxpayer's signature (electronic allowed)
• Representative's signature and declaration

**Doesn't Grant:**
• Right to cash refund checks
• Power to sign return (need separate power)

sections.title: Form 8821: Tax Information Authorization
sections.content:
**What Form 8821 Authorizes:**

• Designee can RECEIVE and INSPECT confidential tax information
• Does NOT authorize representation

**Common Uses:**
• Third parties who need tax information (lenders, attorneys)
• Obtaining transcripts for a client
• When you only need information, not representation

**Key Difference from 2848:**
No authority to represent or advocate for taxpayer

sections.title: CAF Number
sections.content:
**Centralized Authorization File (CAF):**

• Unique number assigned to representatives
• Links you to all clients in IRS system
• Include on all POA forms
• Allows IRS to verify your authorization

**How to Get CAF Number:**
• Request when first filing Form 2848
• Or submit Form 2848 to CAF unit
• Assigned automatically and returned to you
• Keep it - you use it for your entire career!

sections.title: Revoking or Replacing POA
sections.content:
**Taxpayer Revocation:**
• Written statement to IRS
• Copy to representative
• Effective upon receipt

**Representative Withdrawal:**
• Written statement to IRS
• Copy to taxpayer
• Effective upon receipt

**New POA Replaces Old:**
Filing new Form 2848 for same tax matter automatically revokes prior POA unless 'add' is specified.

**Death of Taxpayer:**
POA terminates upon taxpayer's death - executor must grant new authorization.

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Signing Authority
sections.content:
**Form 2848 Does NOT Give Authority to Sign Returns!**

Separate authorization required to sign a return for a taxpayer:
• Specified on Form 2848, or
• Separate power of attorney document, or
• Court-appointed authority

**Exception:** Practitioner must sign the return if they're not appearing as representative (as preparer).

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Form 2848 = Power of Attorney (representation rights)
Form 8821 = Information only (no representation)
CAF number identifies you in IRS authorization system
New POA for same matter revokes prior POA (unless added)
Signing authority requires explicit grant
POA terminates upon taxpayer's death
Taxpayer or representative can revoke at any time

title: Circular 230: Duties and Restrictions
description: Understand the ethical requirements governing practice before the IRS

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Circular 230 is the Bible of EA practice! These Treasury regulations govern everything you do as a practitioner. Violations can result in suspension or disbarment. Know these rules cold - they're heavily tested!

sections.title: Subpart A: Rules Governing Authority
sections.content:
**Key Subpart A Provisions:**

• Who may practice before the IRS
• Enrollment and renewal requirements
• PTIN requirements
• Continuing education requirements

**EA Requirements:**
• 72 hours of CE every 3-year period
• Minimum 16 hours per year
• 2 hours of ethics per year
• 6 hours of federal tax law updates per cycle

sections.title: Subpart B: Duties Relating to Practice
sections.content:
**Key Duties (Section 10.22):**

**Due Diligence:**
• Must exercise due diligence in preparing returns
• Determining correctness of representations to IRS
• Determining correctness of representations to clients

**Must Know or Have Good Faith Belief:**
Positions taken have realistic possibility of being sustained if challenged.

sections.title: Client Records - Section 10.28
sections.content:
**Must Return to Client Upon Request:**
• Records necessary for client to comply with tax obligations
• Include all client-provided records

**May Retain:**
• Copies of records you created/own
• Work product (if fee paid)
• Documents you prepared (if fee paid)

**Fee Dispute Does NOT Justify:**
Refusing to return client records necessary for compliance

**State laws may provide additional protections or requirements**

sections.title: ⚠️ When Client Makes an Error
sections.content:
**Section 10.21 - Knowledge of Client's Omission:**

If you discover a client's error or omission:

1. **Must advise client** promptly of:
   • The noncompliance, error, or omission
   • The consequences

2. **Cannot prepare** current year return ignoring known prior error

3. **Client decides** whether to correct - you cannot force them

4. **You must decide** whether to continue representation

sections.title: Restrictions on Practice
sections.content:
**Section 10.27 - Fees:**
• No unconscionable fees
• No contingent fees (with exceptions)
• Exceptions: IRS exam/challenge, judicial proceedings, refund claims

**Section 10.29 - Conflicting Interests:**
• May not represent if conflict of interest
• Unless all parties consent after full disclosure
• And you reasonably believe competent representation possible

**Section 10.35 - Competence:**
• May not accept representation unless competent or become competent

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Circular 230 governs all practice before the IRS
Due diligence required in all aspects of practice
Client records must be returned upon request
Advise client of errors but client decides whether to correct
No contingent fees except for examinations, claims, litigation
Conflicts of interest require disclosure and consent
72 hours CE per 3-year cycle (minimum 16/year, 2 ethics/year)

title: Tax Return Preparer Penalties
description: Understand the penalties that apply to tax return preparers

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Tax preparers face significant penalties for errors and misconduct. Understanding these penalties helps you maintain a compliant practice and avoid personal liability. These penalties are frequently tested!

sections.title: Section 6694 Penalties
rows: Greater of $1,000 or 50% of income from return
Position not supported by substantial authority (or not disclosed)
Greater of $5,000 or 75% of income from return
Intentional disregard of rules or reckless/intentional disregard of rules

sections.title: Section 6694(a): Unreasonable Position
sections.content:
**Applies when:**
Paid preparer takes a position that understates tax liability.

**Standards:**
• Undisclosed position: Must have "substantial authority"
• Disclosed position: Must have "reasonable basis"
• Tax shelter/reportable transaction: "More likely than not" standard

**Penalty: Greater of $1,000 or 50% of preparer income from return**

**Defenses:**
• Reasonable cause AND good faith reliance on information
• Adequate disclosure (for some positions)

sections.title: Section 6694(b): Willful/Reckless Conduct
sections.content:
**Applies when:**
• Willful attempt to understate tax, OR
• Reckless or intentional disregard of rules/regulations

**Penalty: Greater of $5,000 or 75% of preparer income from return**

**This is more serious:**
• No good faith defense available
• Must prove no willful or reckless conduct
• Often leads to Circular 230 proceedings

sections.title: Section 6695 Penalties
rows: Failure to furnish copy to taxpayer
Must give copy at time of signing
Failure to retain copy or list
Failure to file correct info return

sections.title: ⚠️ Due Diligence Penalty - Form 8867
sections.content:
**Section 6695(g) - $635 Penalty (2024):**

Applies for each failure regarding:
• Earned Income Credit
• Child Tax Credit / ACTC
• American Opportunity Credit
• Head of Household filing status

**Requirements:**
• Complete Form 8867 for each return
• Meet knowledge requirement
• Make reasonable inquiries
• Retain records 3 years

**A return claiming all 4 = potential $2,540 penalty per return!**

sections.title: Avoiding Preparer Penalties
content: Document your due diligence thoroughly
Ask probing questions when information seems incorrect
Disclose aggressive positions properly
Never sign a blank return or return you haven't reviewed
Include your PTIN on every return
Provide copy of return to client at signing
Retain copies of returns for 3 years
Never negotiate or endorse client refund checks

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: 6694(a): $1,000 or 50% of fee for unreasonable positions
6694(b): $5,000 or 75% of fee for willful/reckless conduct
6695: Various $60-$635 penalties for procedural failures
Due diligence penalty: $635 for each of 4 credit/status items
Good faith reliance is a defense to 6694(a)
Never negotiate or endorse client refund checks
Document, document, document!

title: Sanctions and Disciplinary Proceedings
description: Understand the discipline process under Circular 230

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The Office of Professional Responsibility (OPR) can suspend or disbar practitioners for violations. Understanding what constitutes disreputable conduct and the disciplinary process protects your career.

sections.title: Disreputable Conduct (Section 10.51)
sections.content:
**Examples of Disreputable Conduct:**

• Conviction of a crime under federal tax laws
• Conviction of a felony involving dishonesty or breach of trust
• Giving false or misleading information to Treasury Department
• Soliciting clients using false or misleading representations
• Willfully failing to file own tax returns
• Willfully evading taxes
• Misappropriating or failing to properly remit funds
• Directly or indirectly giving false or misleading information in connection with any IRS matter

sections.title: Additional Disreputable Conduct
content: Solicitation of employment involving false statements
Willfully failing to sign a return when required
Willfully assisting in violation of any requirements imposed under the tax laws
Making false accusations against IRS employees
Knowingly counseling clients to violate tax laws
Contemptuous conduct before the IRS
Being disbarred or suspended from practice by a state authority

sections.title: Available Sanctions
rows: Fine up to gross income from matter

sections.title: Disciplinary Proceeding Process
sections.content:
**1. Complaint Filed:**
OPR receives complaint or discovers potential violation.

**2. Investigation:**
OPR investigates and may request statement from practitioner.

**3. Complaint Issued:**
If probable cause found, formal complaint issued.

**4. Answer Filed:**
Practitioner must respond within 30 days.

**5. Hearing:**
Before Administrative Law Judge (ALJ).

**6. Decision:**
ALJ issues decision, appealable to Treasury.

sections.title: Expedited Suspension
sections.content:
**Available for Immediate Action:**

• Practitioner has had state license to practice suspended or revoked
• Practitioner fails to comply with federal tax obligation
• Practitioner poses threat to public

**Process:**
• Practitioner notified and given opportunity to respond
• Summary proceeding
• Suspension effective immediately upon decision

**Why expedited?** Protect the public from imminent harm.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Disreputable conduct includes conviction, false statements, failure to file
Sanctions range from censure to disbarment
Monetary penalties can equal gross income from the matter
OPR handles all disciplinary proceedings
30 days to answer a formal complaint
Expedited suspension for license revocation or immediate threat
Disbarment requires minimum 5 years before reinstatement

title: Circular 230 - Conflicts of Interest
description: Understand when conflicts of interest arise and how to handle them

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Conflicts of interest can undermine your representation and subject you to discipline! Understanding when conflicts arise and how to properly address them protects you and your clients.

sections.title: What is a Conflict of Interest?
sections.content:
**A conflict exists when:**

• Representation of one client will be directly adverse to another client, OR
• There is significant risk representation will be materially limited by:
  - Practitioner's responsibilities to another client
  - Former client
  - Third person
  - Personal interest of the practitioner

sections.title: Representation Despite Conflict
sections.content:
**May represent with conflict IF:**

1. Practitioner reasonably believes competent and diligent representation is possible
2. Representation is not prohibited by law
3. Each affected client gives informed consent confirmed in writing within 30 days

**All three conditions must be met!**

sections.title: Common Conflict Situations
content: Representing both spouses when divorce pending
Representing partnership and individual partner
Representing buyer and seller in same transaction
Representing employer and employee in same matter
Representing multiple taxpayers on same return position
Personal financial interest in client's matter

sections.title: ⚠️ Conflicts That Cannot Be Waived
sections.content:
**Some conflicts cannot be waived even with consent:**

• Practitioner reasonably cannot provide competent representation
• Representation is prohibited by law
• Representing opposing parties in same IRS proceeding

**When in doubt, decline the representation!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Conflict arises when interests of clients or practitioner diverge
Can represent with conflict if all three conditions met
Written consent required within 30 days
Some conflicts cannot be waived
When in doubt, decline or refer

title: Circular 230 - Fees and Fee Restrictions
description: Understand proper fee arrangements and prohibited contingent fees

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Fee arrangements are regulated under Circular 230. Contingent fees are generally prohibited for most IRS matters! Understanding the rules protects you from discipline.

sections.title: Unconscionable Fees
sections.content:
**Prohibited: Charging unconscionable fees**

**Factors considered:**
• Time and labor required
• Novelty and difficulty of questions
• Skill required
• Fee customarily charged for similar services
• Amount involved and results obtained
• Experience, reputation, and ability of practitioner
• Whether fee is fixed or contingent

sections.title: Contingent Fees - General Rule
sections.content:
**Contingent Fee:**
Fee based on whether a specific result is attained (e.g., percentage of refund).

**Generally PROHIBITED for:**
• Preparing original tax returns
• Advising on matters before IRS

**Why prohibited:**
• Might encourage aggressive positions
• Creates conflict between practitioner and client

sections.title: Contingent Fees - Exceptions
sections.content:
**Contingent fees ARE permitted for:**

• Examination of return or refund claim (IRS dispute)
• Claim for credit or refund AFTER return filed
• Any matter before IRS that is primarily a JUDICIAL matter
• Whistleblower claims under Section 7623

**Key:** Audit representation and amended returns allow contingent fees.

sections.title: Fee Documentation
sections.content:
**Best Practices:**

• Written engagement letter
• Clear fee terms and billing method
• Scope of services defined
• Client signature acknowledging
• Retainer terms if applicable

**Protects both practitioner and client!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Unconscionable fees prohibited
Contingent fees generally prohibited for return preparation
Contingent fees allowed for audits and refund claims (after filing)
Whistleblower claims: contingent fees permitted
Written engagement letters recommended

title: Preparer Penalties - Section 6694
description: Understand preparer penalties for understated tax positions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Preparer penalties can be substantial! Understanding the standards for tax positions and defenses against penalties is critical for every tax professional.

sections.title: Section 6694(a): Unreasonable Positions
sections.content:
**Penalty applies if:**

Preparer takes a position that results in understatement AND:
• Position has no substantial authority (undisclosed), OR
• Position has no reasonable basis (disclosed)

**Penalty Amount:**
Greater of $1,000 OR 50% of preparer income from the return

**Standard:** Must have substantial authority (or disclose with reasonable basis)

sections.title: Section 6694(b): Willful or Reckless
sections.content:
**Penalty applies if understatement due to:**

• Willful attempt to understate tax, OR
• Reckless or intentional disregard of rules/regulations

**Penalty Amount:**
Greater of $5,000 OR 75% of preparer income from the return

**Much more serious!**
Bad faith or ignoring known rules.

sections.title: Reasonable Cause Defense
sections.content:
**Avoid penalty if:**

• Reasonable cause for the position, AND
• Preparer acted in good faith

**Defense factors:**
• Normal office practice
• Taxpayer furnished necessary information
• Reliance on professional advice
• Reasonable interpretation of law
• Nature of error

sections.title: ⚠️ Who is a Preparer?
sections.content:
**Tax Return Preparer:**
Any person who prepares all or a substantial portion of any return or claim for refund.

**Includes:**
• Signing preparer
• Non-signing preparers who prepare substantial portions
• Certain advisors whose advice is used

**Excludes:**
• People who just furnish typing or clerical work
• Employees who prepare returns for employer

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: 6694(a): $1,000 or 50% for unreasonable positions
6694(b): $5,000 or 75% for willful/reckless conduct
Substantial authority needed for undisclosed positions
Disclosure + reasonable basis is alternative
Reasonable cause and good faith is defense
Both signing and non-signing preparers can be penalized

title: Preparer Penalties - Section 6695
description: Understand administrative preparer penalties and requirements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Section 6695 penalties cover administrative failures - not signing returns, failing to keep records, etc. These add up quickly and are easily avoided with proper procedures!

sections.title: Section 6695 Penalties (2024)
rows: Failure to furnish copy to taxpayer
Failure to file correct information return

sections.title: PTIN Requirements
sections.content:
**All paid preparers must:**

• Obtain PTIN before preparing returns
• Include PTIN on all returns prepared
• Renew PTIN annually
• $11 fee for initial/renewal (2024)

**Failure to use valid PTIN:**
$60 penalty per return (max $30,000/year)

sections.title: Signing and Copy Requirements
sections.content:
**Signing:**
• Signing preparer must sign in designated space
• Can use electronic signature
• $60 penalty for each failure

**Copies:**
• Must furnish copy to taxpayer
• Must retain copy or list for 3 years
• List must include taxpayer name, TIN, year, type

**Cannot sign blank returns** for later completion!

sections.title: Due Diligence Requirements (Section 6695(g))
sections.content:
**$635 penalty for each failure** (2024) for:

• Earned Income Credit
• Child Tax Credit / Additional CTC
• American Opportunity Tax Credit
• Head of Household filing status

**Four requirements:**
1. Complete Form 8867 (Due Diligence Checklist)
2. Complete applicable worksheets
3. Make reasonable inquiries
4. Retain records for 3 years

**Potential $2,540 penalty on one return!**

sections.title: Refund Check Prohibition
sections.content:
**$600 penalty per check:**

Preparers CANNOT endorse or negotiate (deposit) refund checks for clients.

**Why:** Prevents preparers from taking refund as payment.

**Exception:** NOT a penalty to be listed on direct deposit (bank product).

**This is per check, not per year!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Most 6695 penalties: $60 each, max $30,000/year
Due diligence penalty: $635 per credit/status
Must have valid PTIN to prepare for compensation
Must sign, provide copy, retain records (3 years)
Cannot negotiate refund checks ($600 each)
All penalties can be avoided with proper procedures!

title: Circular 230 - Records and Client Communications
description: Understand record retention, client communication, and information return requirements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Proper record keeping and communication with clients are professional obligations. Understanding what to keep, for how long, and what to return to clients avoids discipline issues!

sections.title: Return of Client Records
sections.content:
**Must Return Upon Request:**

• All records originally provided by client
• Needed by client to comply with tax obligations

**May Retain:**
• Own work product until fees paid
• Copies of returns prepared
• Working papers (generally)

**Cannot hold client's original records hostage for fees!**

sections.title: Practitioner Records Retention
sections.content:
**Required to Keep for 3 Years:**

• Copy of return OR list of returns prepared
• List includes: taxpayer name, TIN, tax year, type of return
• Due diligence documentation (Form 8867)
• Supporting worksheets and documents

**Best Practice:** Keep copies 7+ years (statute considerations).

sections.title: Prompt Disposition of Matters
sections.content:
**Circular 230 requires:**

• Not unreasonably delay matter pending before IRS
• Notify client of status and progress
• Complete agreed work in reasonable time

**Client Communication:**
• Keep clients informed
• Return calls and correspondence reasonably
• Provide copies of IRS correspondence

sections.title: Advertising Standards
sections.content:
**Must NOT:**

• Make false or misleading statements
• Claim fee comparison unless verifiable
• Imply endorsement by IRS

**Professional Designation Use:**
• May use EA, CPA, Attorney, etc.
• Must only use if actually hold designation
• AFSP participants may use specific language only

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Return client's original records upon request
Keep copies/lists for 3 years minimum
Cannot delay matters unreasonably
Keep client informed of case status
No false or misleading advertising
Can use professional designations if held

title: Taxpayer Rights - Taxpayer Bill of Rights
description: Understand the fundamental rights of taxpayers in dealing with the IRS

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: As an EA, you must understand taxpayer rights to effectively represent your clients! The Taxpayer Bill of Rights is foundational to all IRS interactions.

sections.title: The 10 Taxpayer Rights
content: Right to Be Informed
Right to Quality Service
Right to Pay No More Than the Correct Amount
Right to Challenge IRS Position and Be Heard
Right to Appeal an IRS Decision in an Independent Forum
Right to Finality
Right to Privacy
Right to Confidentiality
Right to Retain Representation
Right to a Fair and Just Tax System

sections.title: Key Rights in Practice
sections.content:
**Right to Be Informed:**
• Know what is needed to comply
• Be informed of IRS decisions

**Right to Challenge:**
• Raise objections
• Provide additional documentation

**Right to Appeal:**
• Independent review by Appeals Office
• Right to go to court

**Right to Representation:**
• May have EA, CPA, or Attorney represent
• Can suspend interview to consult

sections.title: Taxpayer Advocate Service
sections.content:
**Independent Organization within IRS:**

**Can help when:**
• Taxpayer facing significant hardship
• IRS systems not working properly
• Issue not resolved through normal channels
• Action needed to prevent irreparable harm

**How to contact:**
• Form 911 (Request for Taxpayer Advocate Service)
• Local Taxpayer Advocate office
• Toll-free number

sections.title: Publication 1 - Your Rights as a Taxpayer
sections.content:
**Must be provided:**

• At start of examination
• With first notice of taxes owed
• When taxpayer asks

**Contains:**
• Summary of taxpayer rights
• Examination, appeal, and collection processes
• Refund information
• Taxpayer Advocate contact information

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: 10 fundamental taxpayer rights codified
Right to representation at any IRS meeting
Right to appeal IRS decisions
Taxpayer Advocate helps with hardship situations
Publication 1 must be provided at examination start
Rights apply to all taxpayers at all times

title: E-file Requirements and Procedures
description: Understand mandatory e-filing requirements for tax professionals

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Electronic filing is now required for most tax professionals. Understanding the rules, ERO responsibilities, and signature requirements is essential for practice!

sections.title: Mandatory E-file Threshold
sections.content:
**If you file 11+ returns in a calendar year:**

• Must file ALL individual returns electronically
• Includes any returns covered by e-file program

**Exceptions:**
• Return rejected and cannot be resubmitted
• Return not accepted by IRS e-file
• Taxpayer opts out (Form 8948)

Threshold used to be much higher - lowered significantly!

sections.title: Electronic Return Originator (ERO)
sections.content:
**ERO Responsibilities:**

• Accept returns for electronic filing
• Verify taxpayer identification
• Retain required forms and signatures
• Submit returns timely
• Comply with all IRS e-file rules

**Must apply to IRS e-file program** before filing.

sections.title: Signature Requirements
rows: Prior year AGI or prior year PIN

sections.title: Form 8879 - IRS e-file Signature Authorization
sections.content:
**Required for Practitioner PIN method:**

• Taxpayer signs before transmission
• ERO retains original or electronic copy
• Retain for 3 years from return due date
• Contains taxpayer consent and PIN

**Cannot transmit without signed Form 8879!**

sections.title: ERO Retention Requirements
sections.content:
**Must Retain 3 Years:**

• Form 8879 (signature authorization)
• Form 8878 (extension authorization)
• Copy of return or summary
• Any supporting documentation

**Electronic or paper retention acceptable.**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Must e-file if preparing 11+ returns
ERO has specific responsibilities for e-filing
Form 8879 required for practitioner PIN method
Retain e-file documents for 3 years
Taxpayer can opt out with Form 8948
Apply for IRS e-file program before filing

title: IRS Audit Process
description: Understand the examination process and how to represent clients during audits

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Representing clients during audits is a core EA function! Understanding how audits work, what the IRS can and cannot do, and how to protect your clients is essential to effective representation.

sections.title: How Returns Are Selected for Audit
sections.content:
**DIF Score (Discriminant Information Function):**
• Computer scoring based on statistical norms
• Higher score = higher probability of change

**Other Selection Methods:**
• Random selection (National Research Program)
• Related examinations (partner, spouse, subsidiary)
• Information matching (W-2, 1099 mismatches)
• Tips and whistleblowers
• Prior audit adjustments
• Specific compliance projects

sections.title: Correspondence Audit
sections.content:
**Handled entirely by mail:**

• Most common type of audit
• Limited issues (itemized deductions, EIC, etc.)
• Reply within 30 days
• Submit documentation by mail

**Key:** Read the letter carefully - they're asking for specific items. Provide ONLY what's requested and keep copies of everything sent.

sections.title: Office and Field Audits
sections.content:
**Office Audit:**
• Report to IRS office with records
• Usually more complex returns
• Multiple issues may be covered
• Direct interaction with examiner

**Field Audit:**
• Agent comes to taxpayer's location
• Business returns, complex returns
• May include tour of business
• Multiple interviews possible

**Your Role:** Attend with client (or instead of client), provide documents, answer questions, negotiate issues.

sections.title: Taxpayer Rights During Audit
content: Right to representation by an authorized practitioner
Right to recording of interviews (with advance notice)
Right to know why IRS is asking for information
Right to know how information will be used
Right to disagree and request supervisor conference
Right to appeal within IRS
Right to fair and impartial examination

sections.title: ⚠️ What to Do and Not Do in an Audit
sections.content:
**DO:**
• Be prepared and organized
• Answer only what's asked
• Provide copies, not originals
• Keep a record of all interactions
• Be professional and courteous

**DON'T:**
• Volunteer information not requested
• Guess or speculate
• Let client answer questions you haven't prepared them for
• Argue about the law during the exam
• Lose your temper

sections.title: Audit Outcomes
sections.content:
**No Change:**
Examiner agrees with return as filed.

**Agreed:**
Taxpayer agrees with proposed changes.
• Sign Form 870 (waiver of restrictions)
• Assessment and collection can proceed

**Unagreed:**
Taxpayer disagrees with proposed changes.
• Request manager conference
• Pursue Appeals
• Eventually may go to court

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: DIF score is primary audit selection method
Three main audit types: correspondence, office, field
Taxpayers have right to representation during audits
Provide only what's requested - don't volunteer
Keep copies of everything provided to IRS
Can disagree and pursue Appeals if not resolved
Form 870 is signed when taxpayer agrees to changes

title: IRS Appeals Process
description: Learn how to effectively use the IRS Appeals process

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Appeals is where most tax disputes are resolved! Understanding how to access Appeals and negotiate effectively can save clients thousands of dollars while avoiding costly litigation.

sections.title: IRS Appeals Mission
sections.content:
**Independent Office:**
Appeals is independent from the examination function.

**Mission:**
Resolve tax controversies without litigation where possible, in a way that's fair and impartial to both the government and the taxpayer.

**Key Difference:**
• Examiners apply the law strictly
• Appeals considers hazards of litigation
• Settlement authority exists in Appeals

sections.title: Getting to Appeals
sections.content:
**After Examination:**

1. Receive 30-day letter with proposed changes
2. File protest (or small case request) within 30 days
3. Case transferred to Appeals
4. Appeals conference scheduled

**Small Case Request:**
• For total proposed change ≤ $25,000 per period
• Brief written request instead of formal protest

**Formal Protest Required:**
• For proposed change > $25,000 per period
• Must include specific elements

sections.title: Elements of a Formal Protest
content: Statement that you want to appeal the findings
Your name, address, and daytime phone
Copy of the 30-day letter you're protesting
Tax periods or years involved
List of changes you disagree with
Statement of facts supporting your position
Statement of law supporting your position
Penalties of perjury statement (signed under penalties of perjury)

sections.title: Appeals Conference
sections.content:
**How It Works:**

• Usually informal discussion
• In person, by phone, or video
• You present your case
• Appeals Officer evaluates "hazards of litigation"
• Settlement negotiated if possible

**Hazards of Litigation:**
Likelihood each party might win or lose if case went to court.

**Example:** If 50% chance IRS loses, they might settle at 50% of proposed tax.

sections.title: Appeals Outcomes
rows: Agree on some issues, may continue on others
If already in Tax Court, Appeals may still resolve

sections.title: Collection Due Process (CDP) Appeals
sections.content:
**Different from Exam Appeals:**

• Challenge collection action (lien or levy)
• Request within 30 days of notice
• Right to court review if no agreement
• Independent review of proposed action

**Issues That Can Be Raised:**
• Appropriateness of collection action
• Collection alternatives (OIC, IA)
• Spousal defenses
• Underlying liability (if no prior opportunity)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Appeals is independent from examination
30 days to file protest after 30-day letter
Small case request for ≤ $25,000 per period
Formal protest requires specific elements and perjury statement
Appeals considers 'hazards of litigation' in settlement
Form 870-AD finalizes Appeals agreement
CDP Appeals available for collection actions

title: Collection Procedures
description: Master IRS collection actions and taxpayer remedies

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: When clients can't pay their tax bills, you become their advocate with Collections! Understanding liens, levies, and payment alternatives is crucial to protecting clients from aggressive collection action.

sections.title: Lien vs. Levy
rows: Secures tax debt; doesn't take property

sections.title: Federal Tax Lien
sections.content:
**When Lien Arises:**
• Tax assessed
• Notice and demand sent
• Taxpayer fails to pay within 10 days

**Lien attaches to ALL property:**
• Real property
• Personal property
• Rights to property
• After-acquired property

**Notice of Federal Tax Lien (NFTL):**
Public filing that gives priority over subsequent purchasers and creditors.

sections.title: Levy
sections.content:
**IRS can seize:**
• Bank accounts
• Accounts receivable
• Wages (ongoing levy)
• Personal property
• Real property (requires court approval for residence)

**Property Exempt from Levy:**
• Necessary clothing and school books
• Fuel, provisions, furniture (limited amounts)
• Books and tools for trade (limited)
• Unemployment benefits
• Workers' comp
• Minimum exemption for wages
• Certain pension and annuity payments

sections.title: Installment Agreements
sections.content:
**Types of IAs:**

**Guaranteed IA:**
• Tax ≤ $10,000
• Filed all returns for 5 years
• Not had IA in prior 5 years
• Can full pay in 3 years or less

**Streamlined IA:**
• Tax ≤ $50,000 (or $100,000 in some cases)
• Can full pay in 72 months (or within CSED)
• No financial statement required

**Regular IA:**
• Any balance
• Requires financial statement (433-F or 433-A)
• Based on collection potential

sections.title: Offer in Compromise
sections.content:
**Grounds for OIC:**

**1. Doubt as to Collectibility (most common):**
Can't pay full amount before CSED expires.

**2. Doubt as to Liability:**
Dispute whether tax is actually owed.

**3. Effective Tax Administration:**
Collection would create economic hardship or be unfair.

**Key Formula (Doubt as to Collectibility):**
Offer Amount = RCP (Reasonable Collection Potential)
= Future Income + Net Equity in Assets

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Collection Statute
sections.content:
**Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED):**

• IRS has **10 years** from assessment to collect
• After CSED, tax is legally uncollectible
• Cannot lien or levy

**CSED can be:** Extended by agreement, OIC, bankruptcy, innocent spouse proceedings, certain other events.

**Key:** Calculate how much time is left before recommending collection alternatives!

sections.title: Currently Not Collectible Status
sections.content:
**When CNC Applies:**
IRS determines taxpayer cannot pay any amount toward tax liability.

**Effect:**
• Collection activity suspended
• Lien may still be filed
• Case reviewed periodically
• Stays in CNC until financial situation changes or CSED expires

**How to Request:**
• Provide financial information
• Show expenses exceed income
• No ability to pay anything

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Lien = claim against property; Levy = seizure of property
Lien arises automatically when tax unpaid after notice and demand
Some property is exempt from levy (clothing, tools, etc.)
Guaranteed IA for ≤$10,000 payable in 3 years
Streamlined IA for ≤$50,000 payable in 72 months
OIC based on RCP (assets + future income)
Collection statute: 10 years from assessment

title: Penalties and Interest
description: Understand common tax penalties and when abatement is available

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Penalties can add substantially to a client's tax bill! Knowing when penalties apply and how to request abatement can save clients significant money. This is heavily tested on the SEE.

sections.title: Failure to File Penalty (FTF)
sections.content:
**Section 6651(a)(1):**

• **Rate:** 5% per month or partial month
• **Maximum:** 25% of tax due
• **Time:** Late filing (not late payment)

**Minimum Penalty (after 60 days late):**
Lesser of $510 (2024) or 100% of tax due

**Extension:** Timely extension avoids FTF if filed by extended due date

**Reasonable cause defense available**

sections.title: Failure to Pay Penalty (FTP)
sections.content:
**Section 6651(a)(2):**

• **Rate:** 0.5% per month or partial month
• **Maximum:** 25% of tax due
• **Time:** Tax not paid by due date

**Reduction:** If on approved installment agreement, reduced to 0.25%/month

**Combined with FTF:** When both apply, FTF reduced by FTP amount (maximum combined 5%/month for first 5 months)

**Reasonable cause defense available**

sections.title: Accuracy-Related Penalties
sections.content:
**Section 6662 - 20% Penalty:**

• Negligence or disregard of rules
• Substantial understatement of income tax
• Substantial valuation misstatement
• Substantial overstatement of pension liabilities
• Substantial estate/gift tax valuation understatement

**Substantial Understatement:**
• Greater of 10% of correct tax or $5,000 ($10,000 for corps)
• Reduced by amounts with substantial authority or adequate disclosure

**Section 6662(h) - 40% Penalty:**
Gross valuation misstatement

sections.title: First Time Penalty Abatement (FTA)
sections.content:
**Administrative Waiver:**

IRS will abate FTF and/or FTP penalties if:
• No penalties in prior 3 tax years
• All required returns filed or on extension
• All tax paid or arrangement in place

**How to Request:**
• By phone
• In writing
• With audit reconsideration

**Key:** Don't use FTA for a small penalty - save it for when it counts!

sections.title: Reasonable Cause Defense
sections.content:
**What is Reasonable Cause?**
Taxpayer exercised ordinary business care and prudence but could not comply.

**Common Examples:**
• Death or serious illness
• Unavoidable absence
• Fire, casualty, or other disaster
• Unable to obtain records
• Reliance on professional advice (limited)

**NOT Reasonable Cause:**
• Lack of funds (for FTF)
• Ignorance of the law
• Simple forgetfulness

sections.title: Interest
sections.content:
**Interest Runs On:**
• Underpayments from due date until paid
• Overpayments from filing date (or due date if filed early) until refunded

**Rate:** Federal short-term rate + 3% (individual underpayments)

**Cannot Be Abated (generally):**
Unlike penalties, interest rarely abated.

**Exception:** Ministerial or managerial acts by IRS causing delay.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: FTF: 5%/month up to 25%; FTP: 0.5%/month up to 25%
Combined maximum: 47.5%
Accuracy-related penalty: 20% (40% for gross misstatement)
First Time Abatement available - save it for significant penalty
Reasonable cause: ordinary care but couldn't comply
Interest rarely abated - runs from due date
Extensions avoid FTF (for filing) but not FTP

title: IRS Notices and Letters
description: Understand common IRS notices and how to respond effectively

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: As an EA, you'll respond to IRS notices regularly! Understanding notice types, deadlines, and proper responses is fundamental to representation.

sections.title: Common CP Notices (Computerized)
rows: Pay, set up plan, or dispute
Refund applied to other taxes owed

sections.title: Letter Notices (LTR)
sections.content:
**Letter notices generated by employees:**

• LTR 525: General 30-day letter
• LTR 531: 90-day letter (Notice of Deficiency)
• LTR 1058/LT11: Final Notice - Intent to Levy
• LTR 3172: Notice of Federal Tax Lien Filed
• LTR 3219: Statutory Notice of Deficiency

**Critical:** 90-day letters and levy notices have strict deadlines!

sections.title: CP2000 - Underreporter Notice
sections.content:
**Not a formal audit, but:**

• IRS matching program found discrepancy
• Third-party info doesn't match return
• 30 days to respond

**Response Options:**
• Agree: Sign and return, pay balance
• Partially agree: Explain which items correct
• Disagree: Provide documentation

**Key:** Often about unreported 1099s, can be explained.

sections.title: Response Best Practices
content: Respond by the deadline on notice
Use the address/fax on the notice - not general IRS address
Include notice number and taxpayer info on all correspondence
Keep copies of everything sent
Use certified mail for deadlines
Request additional time if needed (usually granted once)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: CP notices are computerized; LTR notices are from employees
CP2000 is matching notice, not formal audit
90-day letter (Statutory Notice) is critical deadline
Always respond by deadline shown on notice
Use certified mail for important responses
Keep copies of all correspondence

title: Collection Alternatives
description: Master payment options for taxpayers who cannot pay in full

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Many clients cannot pay their taxes in full. Understanding collection alternatives helps you protect clients from enforced collection while resolving their liabilities!

sections.title: Streamlined Installment Agreement
sections.content:
**Best option for most clients:**

**Requirements:**
• Owe $50,000 or less (assessed balance)
• Can pay off in 72 months or less
• All required returns filed
• Current tax compliance going forward

**Benefits:**
• No financial disclosure required
• Apply online or by phone
• User fee (reduced for direct debit)

**Key:** Pay down below $50,000 to qualify!

sections.title: Offer in Compromise (OIC)
sections.content:
**Settle for less than full amount:**

**Three Types:**
1. Doubt as to Collectibility (most common)
2. Doubt as to Liability
3. Effective Tax Administration (hardship)

**Basic Formula (DATC):**
Offer = RCP (Reasonable Collection Potential)
RCP = Future Income + Asset Equity

**Must demonstrate inability to full-pay before CSED!**

sections.title: Currently Not Collectible (CNC)
sections.content:
**When taxpayer truly cannot pay:**

• All income needed for necessary expenses
• No ability to make any payment
• Collection suspended (not forgiven)
• CSED continues to run

**Requirements:**
• Full financial disclosure (Form 433-A/B)
• IRS determines inability to pay
• Periodic review of status

**Wait for CSED expiration if tax can't be resolved!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Protecting the Client During Collection
sections.content:
**While working on alternative:**

• Get levy released if causing hardship
• Request Collection Due Process hearing if lien/levy issued
• Don't let statute of limitations expire on requesting relief
• File all required returns (IRS won't work with non-filers)
• Stay current on new liabilities

**Collection doesn't stop automatically - must request!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Guaranteed IA: <$10,000, must accept
Streamlined: <$50,000, no financials, 72 months
OIC: Settle for less, must show can't full-pay
CNC: Collection suspended, CSED runs
All options require filed returns
Stay current on new taxes during agreement

title: Collection Due Process and Appeals
description: Understand taxpayer rights to challenge collection actions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Collection Due Process (CDP) gives taxpayers the right to an independent hearing before (or after) liens and levies! This is a powerful protection you need to know.

sections.title: When CDP Rights Arise
sections.content:
**Federal Tax Lien (LT11/Letter 3172):**
• Right to CDP hearing
• 30 days to request after notice

**Levy (LTR 1058/LT11):**
• Right to CDP hearing
• 30 days to request after notice

**First lien/levy triggers CDP rights.**

Subsequent liens/levies: equivalent hearing only.

sections.title: What Can Be Raised at CDP
content: Appropriateness of collection action
Collection alternatives (IA, OIC, CNC)
Spousal defenses
Underlying tax liability (if no prior opportunity)
Procedural errors by IRS
Whether lien/levy meets legal requirements

sections.title: CDP Process
sections.content:
**Steps:**

1. Receive CDP notice (LT11, Letter 3172, etc.)
2. File Form 12153 within 30 days
3. Case assigned to Settlement Officer
4. Hearing (phone, in-person, or correspondence)
5. Determination letter issued
6. 30 days to petition Tax Court if disagree

**Collection suspended during CDP (except for collection period)!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Critical Deadlines
sections.content:
**MUST file Form 12153 within 30 days for:**

• Collection suspension
• Tax Court review rights
• Statute tolling

**After 30 days:**
• Only equivalent hearing available
• Collection continues
• No Tax Court review

**Calendar this immediately upon receipt of CDP notice!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: CDP: 30 days to request, suspends collection
Equivalent hearing: After 30 days, no suspension
Use Form 12153 for both types
Can raise collection alternatives at hearing
Underlying liability: only if no prior opportunity
Tax Court review only for timely CDP request

title: Statute of Limitations
description: Master the time limits on assessments, refunds, and collections

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Statutes of limitation protect both taxpayers and the IRS. Knowing when time runs out on assessments, refunds, and collections is critical to effective representation and planning.

sections.title: Three Key Statutes
rows: Later of due date or filing date
3 years from filing OR 2 years from payment

sections.title: Assessment Statute (ASED)
sections.content:
**General Rule: 3 years** from later of due date or filing date.

**Extended to 6 years:**
• Omission of gross income > 25% of reported income
• Certain foreign income items

**No Limit (Unlimited):**
• Fraud
• Willful attempt to evade tax
• Failure to file a return

**Extended by Agreement:**
Form 872 (fixed date) or 872-A (open-ended) extends ASED.

sections.title: Refund Statute
sections.content:
**Must claim refund by LATER of:**

• 3 years from date return was filed, OR
• 2 years from date tax was paid

**Amount Limited:**
• If claiming within 3 years: Can get back what was paid within 3 years + extensions
• If claiming within 2 years (but after 3 years from filing): Only what was paid in prior 2 years

**Key:** Missing the deadline = losing the refund forever!

sections.title: Collection Statute (CSED)
sections.content:
**10 years from date of assessment.**

After CSED:
• Tax is legally uncollectible
• Liens release automatically
• Cannot levy or seize property

**CSED Suspended:**
• During bankruptcy
• While OIC pending (+ 30 days)
• While CDP appeal pending (+ 30 days)
• While Innocent Spouse request pending
• Time taxpayer is out of country (6+ months)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Form 872 vs 872-A
sections.content:
**Form 872:** Fixed ending date
• Specifies a particular date ASED expires
• Cannot be revoked
• Plan for specific date

**Form 872-A:** Open-ended
• No specific ending date
• Remains open until terminated
• Either party can terminate (90 days + 60 days notice)

**Key:** Open-ended 872-A can leave case open indefinitely!

sections.title: Protective Refund Claims
sections.content:
**When to File:**
• Statute about to expire
• Issue pending that might affect refund
• Hedge against possible later development

**How to File:**
• Form 1040-X or formal claim
• State specific basis for claim
• Reference pending case/ruling if applicable

**Preserves right to refund** even if issue not yet resolved.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Assessment statute: 3 years (6 years for 25%+ omission, unlimited for fraud)
Refund statute: 3 years from filing OR 2 years from payment
Collection statute: 10 years from assessment
Form 872 extends ASED to fixed date; 872-A is open-ended
Certain events suspend collection statute (bankruptcy, OIC, CDP)
Protective claims preserve refund rights when statute running
No return filed = no statute starts running on assessment

title: Office and Field Examinations
description: Master in-person audit preparation and representation strategies

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: In-person audits require face-time with IRS examiners. Your preparation and representation skills directly impact outcomes. Know how to prepare clients, handle document requests, and navigate the examination process.

sections.title: Pre-Audit Preparation
sections.content:
**Before the Examination:**

1. **Review the Return**
   • Identify potential issues before IRS does
   • Understand each item that may be questioned
   • Locate all supporting documentation

2. **Gather Documentation**
   • Organize by issue and year
   • Make copies - never give originals
   • Identify gaps and address them

3. **Interview the Client**
   • Understand facts behind each item
   • Identify potential problem areas
   • Prepare client for possible questions

sections.title: Office Audit Procedures
sections.content:
**Conducted at IRS Office:**

**Initial Contact:**
• IRS sends appointment letter
• Lists items to be examined
• Specifies documents to bring

**At the Audit:**
• Present only requested documents
• Answer only questions asked
• Don't volunteer information
• Take notes on discussions
• Request breaks if needed

**Your Role as Representative:**
• Attend without client (if permitted and appropriate)
• Control information flow
• Advocate for client's positions

sections.title: Field Audit Procedures
sections.content:
**Conducted at Taxpayer's Location:**

**Why Field Audit?**
• Business examinations
• Complex returns
• Need to inspect books and records on-site
• High-income individuals

**Controlling the Environment:**
• Designate conference room away from operations
• Keep other records out of sight
• Have records organized and ready
• Don't let examiner wander freely

**Revenue Agent vs. Revenue Officer:**
• Revenue Agent: Conducts examinations
• Revenue Officer: Collections (different function)

sections.title: Information Document Requests (IDRs)
sections.content:
**IDR Process:**

• Written request from examiner for documents
• Specifies items needed and deadline
• Must respond timely

**Best Practices:**
• Request clarification if unclear
• Negotiate deadlines if needed
• Provide ONLY what's requested
• Document what you provide
• Keep copies of everything submitted

**Extensions:** Usually granted if requested before deadline

sections.title: ⚠️ Common Audit Mistakes
sections.content:
**Avoid These Errors:**

• Volunteering extra information
• Letting client speak without preparation
• Providing documents not requested
• Missing IDR deadlines
• Being confrontational with examiner
• Failing to document what was discussed

**Remember:** Examiners can expand scope if they find issues you didn't expect!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Office audits at IRS; field audits at taxpayer location
Thorough preparation is critical before any examination
Provide only documents specifically requested
Respond to IDRs timely - request extensions if needed
Representative can often attend without client
Document all discussions and items provided
Don't volunteer information beyond what's asked

title: Information Document Requests
description: Master responding to IRS document requests and protecting privileges

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: IDRs are the IRS's formal method of requesting documents during an examination. How you respond affects the audit's scope, duration, and outcome. Know how to respond strategically and protect privileges.

sections.title: What is an IDR?
sections.content:
**Information Document Request (Form 4564):**

• Formal written request for documents
• Lists specific items needed
• Provides response deadline (usually 10-30 days)
• Part of the permanent examination file

**Types of Requests:**
• Documents (bank statements, receipts, contracts)
• Information (explanations, lists)
• Third-party contacts (may require notice)

sections.title: Responding Best Practices
rows: Leave examiner to guess what you sent

sections.title: Requesting Extensions
sections.content:
**When You Need More Time:**

1. Contact examiner BEFORE deadline
2. Explain reason for delay
3. Propose specific new deadline
4. Document the agreement in writing

**Reasonable Reasons:**
• Volume of documents requested
• Need to obtain from third parties
• Client unavailability
• Natural disaster or emergency

**Unreasonable:** Simply not wanting to comply

sections.title: Privileges and Protections
sections.content:
**Attorney-Client Privilege:**
• For communications with attorneys seeking legal advice
• Does NOT extend to EAs (limited exception below)

**Tax Practitioner Privilege (IRC §7525):**
• Limited privilege for federally authorized practitioners
• Only for non-criminal tax matters
• Does NOT apply to tax shelter advice
• Does NOT protect work papers

**Work Product Doctrine:**
• Protects materials prepared in anticipation of litigation
• May not apply during examination phase

sections.title: What Must Be Produced
sections.content:
**IRS Has Broad Authority:**

• All books and records relevant to tax liability
• Bank statements and cancelled checks
• Contracts and agreements
• Correspondence related to transactions
• Workpapers (generally not privileged)

**Exceptions:**
• Truly privileged communications
• Items not relevant to examination
• Items already in IRS possession

sections.title: ⚠️ Summons Power
sections.content:
**If You Refuse to Comply:**

IRS can issue a **Summons** (IRC §7602):
• Compels production of documents
• Compels testimony under oath
• Enforceable in federal court

**Failure to comply with summons:**
• Contempt of court
• Potential criminal penalties

**Better approach:** Negotiate scope, request extensions, but ultimately comply.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: IDRs are formal document requests - respond timely
Provide only what's specifically requested
Request extensions BEFORE deadline expires
Tax practitioner privilege is limited - doesn't cover work papers
Keep copies and document what you provide
IRS can issue summons if you don't comply
Transmittal letters create clear record of responses

title: Examination Closing Procedures
description: Understand how examinations conclude and client options at each stage

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: How an examination closes determines your client's next steps. Understanding the difference between agreed, unagreed, and no-change closures lets you advise clients on options and prepare for potential appeals.

sections.title: Three Examination Outcomes
rows: Sign Form 870, pay or set up payment plan

sections.title: No Change Closure
sections.content:
**Best Outcome:**

• Examiner found no adjustments
• Return accepted as filed
• Client receives "No Change" letter
• Case is closed

**Keep Records:**
• Retain documentation used in audit
• May be relevant if future years examined
• Shows positions were previously accepted

sections.title: Agreed Cases
sections.content:
**When Client Agrees with Adjustments:**

1. **Examiner prepares Form 4549** (Income Tax Examination Changes)
   • Shows proposed adjustments
   • Calculates additional tax, penalties, interest

2. **Client signs Form 870** (Waiver of Restrictions)
   • Waives requirement for statutory notice (90-day letter)
   • Allows immediate assessment
   • Interest continues until paid

3. **Assessment and Payment**
   • Tax assessed when Form 870 processed
   • Client has options for payment (lump sum, installment)

**Note:** Signing Form 870 generally cannot be undone!

sections.title: Unagreed Cases
sections.content:
**When Client Disagrees:**

1. **Request Manager Conference**
   • Meet with examiner's supervisor
   • May resolve issues at this level

2. **Receive 30-Day Letter**
   • Preliminary notice of proposed changes
   • Right to appeal within 30 days

3. **Choose Response:**
   • Request Appeals conference
   • Accept changes and pay
   • Do nothing (leads to 90-day letter)

4. **If Still Unagreed: Statutory Notice (90-Day Letter)**
   • Formal notice of deficiency
   • 90 days to petition Tax Court

sections.title: Form 870 vs. Form 870-AD
rows: Waives 90-day letter, cannot be reopened except for fraud
Closing agreement, prevents refund claim, prevents IRS reopening

sections.title: ⚠️ When NOT to Agree
sections.content:
**Consider rejecting settlement if:**

• Legal position is strong
• Penalty abatement is warranted but not offered
• Significant dollar amounts involved
• Examiner misunderstands facts or law
• Client willing to pursue Appeals or Tax Court

**Appeals** often reaches better results than examination level!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: No change = case closed with return accepted
Agreed = sign Form 870, waive right to 90-day letter
Unagreed = receive 30-day letter with appeal rights
Manager conference may resolve issues before appeal
Form 870 generally cannot be undone once signed
Form 870-AD at Appeals is a closing agreement
Appeals often achieves better results than examination

title: 30-Day and 90-Day Letters
description: Understand IRS preliminary and statutory notices and response options

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The 30-day and 90-day letters are critical decision points in the examination process. Missing deadlines can eliminate options. Know what each letter means and how to respond.

sections.title: The 30-Day Letter
sections.content:
**Preliminary Notice:**

**What It Is:**
• IRS proposal of changes
• NOT a formal assessment
• Invitation to agree or appeal

**Contains:**
• Examination report (Form 4549)
• Explanation of changes
• Appeal rights information (Pub 5)

**Response Options (within 30 days):**
1. Sign and agree
2. Request Appeals conference
3. Provide additional information
4. Do nothing (leads to 90-day letter)

sections.title: The 90-Day Letter (Statutory Notice of Deficiency)
sections.content:
**The "Ticket to Tax Court":**

**What It Is:**
• Formal statutory notice required before assessment
• Also called "Notice of Deficiency"
• Certified mail to last known address

**Critical Deadline:**
• **90 days** from mailing to petition Tax Court
• **150 days** if addressed outside the US
• Missing deadline = lose Tax Court option!

**Effect:**
• Allows IRS to assess after 90 days
• Freezes assessment during petition period
• Starts prepayment forum jurisdiction

sections.title: Response Options to 90-Day Letter
sections.content:
**Within 90 Days:**

**Option 1: Petition Tax Court**
• Prepay is NOT required
• Get judicial review of proposed deficiency
• Must file timely petition

**Option 2: Agree and Pay**
• Sign and return agreement
• Pay or set up payment plan
• Case closed

**Option 3: Do Nothing**
• After 90 days, IRS assesses tax
• Collection process begins
• Can still contest by paying and filing refund claim

sections.title: Calculating the 90-Day Deadline
sections.content:
**The deadline is statutory - no extensions!**

**Calculate from mailing date:**
• Date on letter (postmark date for processing)
• Add 90 calendar days
• If 90th day is Saturday, Sunday, or holiday, next business day applies

**Example:**
• Letter dated January 15
• 90 days = April 15
• If April 15 is weekend, deadline is next Monday

**The date the petition is FILED (received) matters, not mailing date (usually)!**

**Electronic filing:** Available and encouraged

sections.title: ⚠️ Critical: Address Changes
sections.content:
**IRS sends notice to "last known address":**

• If client moved, may not receive 90-day letter!
• Letter sent to old address is still valid
• 90-day clock still runs

**Protect Clients:**
• File Form 8822 (change of address) promptly
• Consider USPS mail forwarding
• Monitor client addresses in IRS records

**Missed deadline = no Tax Court = must pay first then sue in refund forum!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: 30-day letter is preliminary - invites agreement or appeal
90-day letter is statutory notice required for deficiency
90 days to petition Tax Court - NO EXTENSIONS
Missing 90-day deadline = lose prepayment forum
Sent to last known address - valid even if not received
Small case letter allows informal Appeals for ≤$25,000
Tax Court petition does NOT require prepayment

title: IRS Appeals Process
description: Master the administrative appeals process for tax disputes

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: IRS Appeals resolves most tax disputes without litigation. Appeals officers have authority to settle cases based on hazards of litigation. Knowing how Appeals works lets you achieve better outcomes for clients.

sections.title: What is IRS Appeals?
sections.content:
**Independent Division of IRS:**

• Separate from Examination
• Mission is to resolve disputes fairly
• Settlement authority based on litigation hazards
• Informal process (not like court)

**Key Concept: Hazards of Litigation**
Appeals considers the likelihood that IRS would win in court. If IRS position is weak, they'll settle!

sections.title: How to Request Appeals
rows: Detailed statement of facts and law

sections.title: Written Protest Requirements
sections.content:
**For amounts over $25,000, include:**

1. Statement requesting Appeals conference
2. Your name, address, and contact information
3. Copy of the letter showing proposed changes
4. Tax periods or years involved
5. Statement of facts (in your words, not IRS's)
6. Discussion of applicable law
7. Argument why you disagree
8. Penalty of perjury statement

**Submit within the time stated in IRS letter** (usually 30 days)

sections.title: The Appeals Conference
sections.content:
**What to Expect:**

• Informal meeting (in-person, video, or phone)
• Present your case and arguments
• Appeals Officer considers both sides
• May request additional documentation
• Has settlement authority

**Preparation:**
• Organize legal arguments by issue
• Identify strengths and weaknesses
• Know your "walk away" point
• Prepare client for possible outcomes
• Bring documentation to support positions

sections.title: Settlement Strategies
sections.content:
**Effective Approaches:**

**1. Focus on hazards of litigation**
• Show where IRS position is legally weak
• Cite relevant case law

**2. Split the baby**
• Propose splitting contested amounts
• Shows reasonableness

**3. Trade issues**
• Concede weak issues
• Fight strong issues harder

**4. Penalty abatement**
• Often available even when tax sustained
• Reasonable cause arguments

**Appeals often achieves 20-50% reductions!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Ex Parte Communication Rules
sections.content:
**Appeals must remain independent:**

• Prohibited from certain communications with Exam
• Cannot discuss specific facts of case with examiner
• Can only discuss legal interpretations

**Why it matters:**
• You get fresh look at case
• Appeals isn't bound by examiner's views
• Different decision-maker may see it differently

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Appeals is independent from Examination
Settlement based on hazards of litigation
Small cases (≤$25K): Brief letter request
Larger cases: Formal written protest required
Focus arguments on where IRS is legally weak
Appeals often achieves significant reductions
Penalty abatement commonly available at Appeals

title: Return Filing Requirements
description: Master who must file and when for various return types

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Knowing who must file and when is fundamental! Penalties apply for late or missing returns. As a practitioner, ensuring clients meet filing obligations is one of your core responsibilities.

sections.title: Must File Regardless of Income
sections.content:
**Special filing requirements:**

• Self-employment income ≥ $400
• Social Security/Medicare tax on tips not reported to employer
• Advance Premium Tax Credit received
• Health Savings Account distributions
• Alternative minimum tax owed
• Household employment taxes owed
• Recapture taxes (first-time homebuyer, education)
• Write-in taxes

**Also file to claim refund or refundable credit!**

sections.title: Information Return Due Dates
sections.content:
**Forms 1099-NEC:** Due January 31 (to IRS and recipients)

**Forms 1099-MISC, 1099-INT, etc.:**
• To recipients: January 31
• To IRS: February 28 (paper) or March 31 (electronic)

**Forms W-2:**
• To employees: January 31
• To SSA: January 31

**E-file mandate:** Required for 10+ information returns

sections.title: Fiscal Year Due Dates
sections.content:
**For non-calendar year taxpayers:**

• Return due 15th day of 4th month after year end
• Extension adds 6 months (5 months for certain entities)

**Example: Fiscal year ending June 30:**
• Original due date: October 15
• Extended due date: April 15 (following year)

**Partnerships/S Corps with fiscal years:**
• Due 15th day of 3rd month after year end

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Individual thresholds vary by filing status and age
Must file with SE income ≥ $400 regardless of threshold
Partnerships/S Corps due March 15 (calendar year)
C Corps/Individuals due April 15 (calendar year)
1099-NEC due January 31 to both IRS and recipients
E-file required for 10+ information returns
File even below threshold if refund or credit available

title: Extensions and Estimated Taxes
description: Master extension procedures and estimated tax safe harbors

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Extensions give clients more time to file without penalty, but payment is still due on time. Understanding estimated tax requirements prevents underpayment penalties throughout the year.

sections.title: Extensions - Key Points
sections.content:
**Extension Extends TIME TO FILE, NOT TIME TO PAY!**

**Automatic upon filing:**
• No reason required
• IRS does not "approve"
• Just file timely and extension is granted

**Payment still due:**
• Estimate tax and pay with extension
• Failure to pay = penalty + interest
• Interest runs from original due date

**Auto-extension for overseas:**
• U.S. citizens/residents abroad: 2-month auto-extension
• Interest still accrues from original due date!

sections.title: Estimated Tax Requirements
sections.content:
**Who Must Pay Estimates:**

• Expected liability ≥ $1,000 after withholding and credits
• Self-employed individuals
• Investors with significant gains/dividends
• Retirees without adequate withholding

**Quarterly Due Dates (Calendar Year):**
• Q1: April 15
• Q2: June 15
• Q3: September 15
• Q4: January 15 (following year)

sections.title: Safe Harbor Rules
sections.content:
**Avoid Penalty by Paying Either:**

**Option 1: Current Year Method**
• 90% of current year's tax liability

**Option 2: Prior Year Method**
• 100% of prior year's tax liability, OR
• 110% if prior year AGI > $150,000 ($75,000 MFS)

**Prior year safe harbor is usually easier** - you know the amount!

**Requirements:**
• Prior year was 12-month tax year
• Prior year had a tax liability

sections.title: Annualized Income Method
sections.content:
**For Uneven Income Throughout Year:**

If income varies seasonally:
• Can annualize income each quarter
• Pay estimates based on income earned through period

**Form 2210, Schedule AI required**

**Useful for:**
• Seasonal businesses
• Capital gains realized in one quarter
• Bonuses received late in year

**Benefit:** Lower early estimates when income is low

sections.title: ⚠️ Underpayment Penalty
sections.content:
**Penalty if you don't meet safe harbors:**

• Interest-based penalty on underpayment
• Calculated quarter by quarter
• IRS or Form 2210 computes amount

**No penalty if:**
• Total tax less than $1,000
• You paid 100% (or 110%) of prior year
• You paid 90% of current year

**Increased withholding = estimated payments!**
Withholding is treated as paid evenly throughout the year.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Form 4868 extends filing to October 15 (individuals)
Extension is automatic - no approval needed
Payment still due by original due date
Estimate safe harbors: 90% current OR 100%/110% prior year
Quarterly estimates: April, June, September, January
Annualized income method for seasonal income variation
Increase W-4 withholding as alternative to estimates

title: Electronic Filing Requirements
description: Understand e-file mandates and preparer requirements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Electronic filing is now the standard. Most preparers must e-file, and clients expect it. Know the mandates, ERO responsibilities, and signature requirements.

sections.title: E-File Mandates
rows: Varies by form and filer size

sections.title: ERO Responsibilities
sections.content:
**Electronic Return Originator (ERO):**

**A preparer who e-files must:**
• Apply to be Authorized IRS e-file Provider
• Complete e-file application
• Pass suitability check
• Comply with Pub 1345 requirements

**ERO Duties:**
• Collect taxpayer signatures (Form 8879)
• Retain signed authorization 3 years
• Provide copy of return to taxpayer
• Correct rejected returns promptly

sections.title: Form 8879: IRS e-file Signature Authorization
sections.content:
**Required Before Transmitting Return:**

• Taxpayer (and spouse) must sign
• Authorizes ERO to enter PIN for e-signature
• Authorizes direct deposit/debit
• Must be signed AFTER return is complete

**Cannot transmit before obtaining signature!**

**Retain Form 8879 for 3 years** from due date or filing date, whichever is later.

sections.title: Electronic Signatures
sections.content:
**Self-Select PIN Program:**
• Taxpayer creates 5-digit PIN
• Used to sign e-filed return
• ERO enters on behalf of taxpayer (with authorization)

**Practitioner PIN:**
• Preparer enters taxpayer's prior-year AGI or PIN
• Creates signature for current return

**Remote Transactions:**
• IRS permits electronic signatures on Form 8879
• Specific requirements apply (email, portal, etc.)
• Made permanent after COVID-era temporary rules

sections.title: Rejected Returns
sections.content:
**If E-File Is Rejected:**

1. Notify taxpayer within 24 hours
2. Take reasonable steps to fix error
3. Re-transmit corrected return

**Common Rejection Reasons:**
• Duplicate SSN (already filed)
• AGI/PIN mismatch
• Incorrect dependent SSN
• Name/SSN mismatch with SSA records

**If can't be fixed:** May file paper return with rejection code

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Preparers filing 11+ returns must e-file
Must be Authorized IRS e-file Provider
Form 8879 required before transmitting
Retain Form 8879 for 3 years
Electronic signatures now permitted on 8879
Fix and re-transmit rejected returns within 24 hours
Paper filing allowed if e-file rejected and uncorrectable

title: Non-Filer Procedures
description: Handle clients who haven't filed required returns

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Many potential clients walk in with years of unfiled returns. Knowing how to bring them into compliance, deal with IRS notices, and challenge substitute returns is essential for building a tax practice.

sections.title: Substitute for Return (SFR)
sections.content:
**What is an SFR?**

If you don't file, IRS can prepare a return FOR you:
• Uses available information (W-2s, 1099s)
• Files as Single or MFS (least favorable)
• No itemized deductions
• No exemptions/credits
• Almost always OVERSTATES tax

**The SFR is assessed, and collection begins!**

sections.title: SFR Process
rows: Petition Tax Court OR file actual return

sections.title: Filing Delinquent Returns
sections.content:
**To Replace an SFR:**

1. Prepare accurate return for the year(s)
2. File with IRS (by mail if e-file rejected)
3. Include all applicable deductions and credits
4. Show correct tax liability

**IRS Processing:**
• Reviews filed return vs. SFR
• Adjusts assessment if return accepted
• May audit filed return

**Time limit:** Generally none to file correcting return, but refunds expire after 3 years from original due date

sections.title: Penalty Relief for Non-Filers
sections.content:
**First Time Abatement:**
• Available if prior 3 years clean
• Covers failure-to-file and failure-to-pay
• Request when filing delinquent returns

**Reasonable Cause:**
• Medical issues, natural disasters
• Death in family
• Reliance on professional
• Document thoroughly!

**File extension for future years** even if not ready to file the return

sections.title: Voluntary Disclosure Practice
sections.content:
**For Serious Non-Compliance:**

**Criminal Investigation Voluntary Disclosure:**
• Pre-clearance required
• Before IRS initiates investigation
• May avoid criminal prosecution
• Civil penalties still apply

**Offshore Voluntary Disclosure:**
• For unreported foreign income/accounts
• Specific procedures apply
• Better than getting caught!

**Consult with specialists** for serious non-filer cases

sections.title: ⚠️ Practice Tips
sections.content:
**Working with Non-Filers:**

• Get ALL prior returns filed before current year
• IRS prioritizes compliance - show good faith
• Request penalty abatement in writing
• Consider Installment Agreement for amounts owed
• Watch refund statutes - they expire!

**Document everything** - these cases often have collection issues too

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: SFR is IRS-prepared return - usually wrong and overstated
SFR uses single/MFS, no deductions, no credits
Can replace SFR by filing accurate return
First Time Abatement available if prior 3 years clean
Refund expires 3 years from original due date
Get all delinquent years filed for compliance
Serious cases may need criminal defense counsel

title: Penalties and Interest
description: Master common penalties, interest calculations, and abatement strategies

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Penalties and interest can exceed the original tax! Knowing the penalty structure, how interest compounds, and strategies for abatement helps you protect clients and manage their liabilities.

sections.title: Failure to File vs. Failure to Pay
sections.content:
**Failure to File (FTF):**
• 5% per month (or part of month)
• Maximum 25%
• Minimum: lesser of $510 or 100% of tax (if 60+ days late)

**Failure to Pay (FTP):**
• 0.5% per month
• Maximum 25%
• Increases to 1%/month after notice demand
• Reduces to 0.25% if on installment agreement

**When Both Apply:**
FTF reduced by FTP for same month (net 4.5%/month + 0.5%/month = 5%)

sections.title: Accuracy-Related Penalties
sections.content:
**20% Penalty for:**

• **Negligence:** Lack of reasonable attempt to comply
• **Disregard:** Careless, reckless, or intentional disregard of rules
• **Substantial Understatement:**
  - Individuals: > $5,000 OR > 10% of correct tax
  - Corporations: > $10 million OR > 10% of correct tax
• **Substantial Valuation Misstatement:** Overstated value by 150%+

**Defense:** Reasonable cause and good faith

sections.title: Interest on Underpayments
sections.content:
**Interest Rate:**
• Federal short-term rate + 3%
• Compounded daily
• Adjusted quarterly
• Typically 7-8% (varies by economy)

**Interest Cannot Be Abated!**
• Only exceptions: IRS error causing delay
• Even with penalty abatement, interest continues

**Interest runs from:**
• Due date of return (or extended due date for payment)
• Assessment date for some penalties

sections.title: Penalty Abatement Strategies
rows: 3 prior years clean, all returns filed

sections.title: Requesting Abatement
sections.content:
**First Time Abatement (FTA):**
• Request by phone (for simple cases)
• Write letter citing FTA administrative waiver
• Applies to FTF and FTP for one year

**Reasonable Cause:**
• Written request explaining circumstances
• Document ordinary business care
• Explain what caused non-compliance
• Show how you tried to comply

**Form 843** for formal refund claim including penalty abatement

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: FTF = 5%/month (max 25%); FTP = 0.5%/month (max 25%)
Combined can reach 47.5% plus interest
Accuracy penalty = 20% for negligence or understatement
Interest cannot be abated (except IRS error)
First Time Abatement if 3 prior years clean
